text: In this video, we will show a robotic ventral rectopexy with the aim to treat a symptomatic rectocele recurrence. We'll report the case of a fifty-five year old woman with a past surgical history of laparoscopic rectopexy according to Wells.

In this video, we will show a robotic ventral rectopexy with the aim to treat a symptomatic rectocele recurrence. We'll report the case of a fifty-five year old woman with a past surgical history of laparoscopic rectopexy according to Wells.

This patient was complaining of the typical symptoms of obstructive defecation syndrome. An extensive clinical workup was ruled out. The balloon expulsion test was normal while anorectal manometry showed a sphincter hypotonia. You can easily see in the MR defecography a large rectocele of four centimeters and an incomplete defecation. The previous mesh can be easily seen in this sequence.

No consensus can be found in the literature regarding the treatment of rectocele recurrence. We opted for a robotic ventral approach using the five ports and four arms robotic technique. We used the da Vinci XI surgical system. The details will be discussed further.

The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon out of the pelvis, exposing the anterior aspect of the promontory on the right side of the sigmoid mesentery using scissors with monopolar cautery. The old mesh is visible, fixed to the right part of the promontory and the lateral part of the middle rectum, illustrating the recurrence of rectal prolapse.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneum over the Douglas pouch, revealing the levator ani muscle. The position of the initial mesh is visually compared to the operator's finger during a digital rectal exam.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneum over the Douglas pouch, revealing the levator ani muscle. The position of the initial mesh is visually compared to the operator's finger during a digital rectal exam, highlighting its high placement.
The surgeon introduces a bioprosthetic collagen porcine mesh and secures it to the anterior aspect of the lower rectum using five non-absorbable polyethylene-coated braided polyester stitches.
A bioprosthetic collagen porcine mesh is introduced and fixed onto the anterior aspect of the lower rectum using five non-absorbable polyethylene-coated braided polyester stitches.

The surgeon fastens the new mesh to the old mesh using two non-absorbable sutures. The promontory is dissected, leaving the old mesh in place. The anterior sacroiliac ligament at the sacral promontory is exposed, and a non-absorbable stitch is used to secure the mesh to the sacrum.

The surgeon fastens the new mesh to the old mesh with two non-absorbable sutures. The promontory is dissected, leaving the old mesh in place. The anterior sacroiliac ligament at the sacral promontory is exposed, and a non-absorbable stitch is used to secure the mesh to the sacrum.

The surgeon closes the Douglas pouch, creating a passage for the sigmoid colon. The peritoneum is then closed over the meshes using two continuous barbed absorbable sutures, effectively isolating the meshes from the abdominal cavity.

In this slide, you can see the differences in the pelvic region before and after the treatment. In conclusion, robot-assisted ventral re-rectopexy is a feasible procedure for the treatment of rectocele recurrence. If needed, the old mesh could be left in place and sutured to the new one.
